hits.name: Cellular tumor antigen p53
hits.function: Multifunctional transcription factor that induces cell cycle arrest, DNA repair or apoptosis upon binding to its target DNA sequence (PubMed:19556538, PubMed:20673990, PubMed:22726440). Acts as a tumor suppressor in many tumor types; induces growth arrest or apoptosis depending on the physiological circumstances and cell type. Negatively regulates cell division by controlling expression of a set of genes required for this process. One of the activated genes is an inhibitor of cyclin-dependent kinases. hits.name: Tumor protein p73
hits.function: Participates in the apoptotic response to DNA damage. Isoforms containing the transactivation domain are pro-apoptotic, isoforms lacking the domain are anti-apoptotic and block the function of p53 and transactivating p73 isoforms. May be a tumor suppressor protein. Is an activator of FOXJ1 expression (By similarity). It is an essential factor for the positive regulation of lung ciliated cell differentiation (PubMed:34077761)

hits.name: Ribosome biogenesis protein NOP53
hits.function: Nucleolar protein which is involved in the integration of the 5S RNP into the ribosomal large subunit during ribosome biogenesis (PubMed:24120868). In ribosome biogenesis, may also play a role in rRNA transcription (PubMed:27729611). Also functions as a nucleolar sensor that regulates the activation of p53/TP53 in response to ribosome biogenesis perturbation, DNA damage and other stress conditions (PubMed:21741933, PubMed:24120868, PubMed:27829214). text:
The P53_HUMAN protein, also known as Cellular tumor antigen p53, is a multifunctional transcription factor that acts as a tumor suppressor in many tumor types [UniProt:P04637].

### Protein Details
* **Identity**: Cellular tumor antigen p53 in *Homo sapiens* [UniProt:P04637].
* **Length**: 393 amino acids [UniProt:P04637].
* **Function**: It induces cell cycle arrest, DNA repair, or apoptosis upon binding to its target DNA sequence [UniProt:P04637]. The specific response (growth arrest or apoptosis) depends on the cell type and physiological circumstances [UniProt:P04637].

### Evidence
* [UniProt:P04637]
